nativeRange.habitat: Widely distributed across permanent freshwater habitats — lakes, ponds, slow rivers, and freshwater lagoons — and extending into tidal freshwater and brackish zones, as far north as the Arctic tree line. Generally found among submerged vegetation and leaf litter where biofilm and detritus accumulate.

decorPreferences: dense clumps of moss (Java moss, Christmas moss)
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak, beech) for shredding and biofilm
driftwood and complex hardscape for cover
sponge filter to avoid sucking up adults and juveniles

diet.notes: Primarily a shredding detritivore — generally documented eating leaf litter, decaying plant matter, biofilm, filamentous algae, and diatoms. Readily accepts supplementary fish flakes, sinking pellets, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables. Has been reported to opportunistically consume soft-bodied invertebrates, fish eggs, and newly-hatched fry, so it tends to function as both a cleanup detritivore and a low-grade micropredator.

goodWith: established planted shrimp tanks (as a self-renewing live-food population)
small peaceful fish that won't eradicate the colony
avoidWith: tanks holding valuable dwarf shrimp larvae (scuds have been reported to predate on shrimplets and eggs in some setups)
fish small enough to be a target rather than a predator (none — scuds are typically prey, not predator, in mixed community tanks)
any tank treated with copper-based medications

summary: The American Freshwater Scud is a tiny North American amphipod, 3–8 mm as an adult, that moves through leaf litter and moss with a characteristic sideways swimming motion. Widely kept as a self-sustaining cleanup crew and live food source, it tolerates a broad range of freshwater parameters and reproduces prolifically on its own.
careNotes: Hyalella azteca is generally considered one of the easiest freshwater invertebrates to keep — it tolerates a wide spectrum of temperature, pH, and hardness, and establishes a self-sustaining colony with minimal intervention so long as leaf litter, moss, or other biofilm-bearing surfaces are present. Hard, moderately alkaline water tends to be preferred. As with all crustaceans, scuds are highly copper-sensitive — avoid copper-based medications and check fertilisers. Reputation as a 'cleanup crew' species is partially deserved (they shred detritus efficiently) but they have been reported to opportunistically nibble dwarf-shrimp eggs and newly-hatched shrimplets, so they should generally be kept out of tanks where dwarf shrimp breeding is a priority.
breedingNotes: Generally considered effortless — colonies expand rapidly without keeper intervention once 20+ starter individuals are introduced into a tank with leaf litter and moss. Males clasp pre-moult females in a behaviour called amplexus (a pre-mating grip common among amphipods); fertilised eggs are carried in a marsupium (brood pouch) until hatching, with around 20–30 eggs per clutch in mature females and an incubation of roughly 11 days at 20°C. Young emerge as fully-formed miniatures (~1 mm) and are immediately independent. Sexual maturity is reached at about three weeks of age (after the sixth moult). Because reproduction is so prolific and the animals are bottom-dwelling, they are commonly cultured in dedicated tubs as a renewable live-food source for small fish and amphibians.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary returned HTTP 403 on access; could not be cross-checked directly. Listed for traceability; substitute primary/secondary sources used below.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on access; could not be cross-checked. Listed for traceability.

additional.name: Tankably — Hyalella azteca care page
additional.notes: Substitute primary. Provided temperature 20–25°C, pH 6–8, GH equivalent 50–270 ppm, adult size 0.3–0.8 cm, lifespan ~1 year, 10 L minimum, detritivore diet, family Hyalellidae / order Amphipoda, peaceful temperament, native range Americas.

additional.name: Phillips Fishworks — Discover Scuds
additional.notes: Cross-check secondary. Confirmed adult size 3–8 mm, lifespan 10–12 months, temperature 45–90°F tolerated with 68–86°F optimal for reproduction, pH 6.5–8, GH 5–15, KH 2–10, 5-gallon minimum tank, sponge filter recommendation, copper sensitivity, and live-food role.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hyalella azteca
additional.notes: Taxonomic + native-range confirmation. Family Hyalellidae, order Amphipoda; distribution across North/Central America and the Caribbean as far north as the Arctic tree line; adult length 3–8 mm with males larger than females; cannot tolerate pH below 6.0; ecologically important detritivore.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Scuds in Shrimp and Fish Tank
additional.notes: Cross-check secondary. Confirmed prolific reproduction (~20–30 eggs per brood, ~11 day incubation at 20°C, maturity ~3 weeks at the sixth moult, neonates ~1 mm), copper sensitivity, and warning that scuds will opportunistically predate on dwarf-shrimp eggs/larvae. Adult size figure (10–20 mm) appears to conflate Hyalella with Gammarus and is not used here.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Hyalella azteca passes through a minimum of nine instars over its short life; juveniles moult frequently (roughly weekly), with each moult tied to growth and — in mature females — to reproductive amplexus (the pre-mating clasping behaviour). Shed exoskeletons are reincorporated into the detritus food web and need not be removed.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Self-sustaining colonies establish from as few as 20 starter individuals. Females carry roughly 20–30 eggs per clutch in a marsupium — a small brood pouch on the underside of the body — and incubation takes around 11 days at 20°C. Young emerge as approximately 1 mm miniatures and are immediately independent. Sexual maturity is reached at about three weeks, after the sixth moult. No special water adjustments or salinity changes are needed to trigger spawning.
